questions.question: In Angular's built-in `@for` block, what is true about the `track` expression?
options: It is mandatory; the compiler errors (NG5002) if you omit it
It is optional and defaults to `track $index`
It is only required when iterating over objects, not primitives
It was removed in v18 in favor of automatic identity diffing
questions.explanation: Unlike the old optional `trackBy`, `@for` makes `track` mandatory and the compiler errors with NG5002 when it is missing, forcing a stable identity for each row.

questions.question: Using `@if`, `@for`, and `@switch` requires importing `CommonModule` (or the equivalent directives) into the component's `imports`.
questions.explanation: The built-in control flow is part of the template language itself, parsed directly by the compiler. There is nothing to import â€” `CommonModule` is no longer needed just for conditionals and loops.

questions.question: How does `@switch` / `@case` compare its value against each case?
options: Strict `===` equality, with no fall-through between cases
Loose `==` equality, with fall-through until a `break`
Reference equality only, ignoring primitive values
Deep structural equality of the case object
questions.explanation: `@switch` uses strict `===` comparison with no fall-through and no `break`; `@default` is optional. There is no loose comparison or fall-through behavior.

questions.question: Which of the following are implicit contextual variables available inside a `@for` block? (select all that apply)
options: `$index`
`$count`
`$last`
`$key`
`$length`
questions.explanation: `@for` exposes `$index`, `$count`, `$first`, `$last`, `$even`, and `$odd`. There is no `$key` or `$length` contextual variable.

questions.question: What distinguishes an **attribute** directive from a **structural** directive?
options: An attribute directive changes appearance/behavior of an existing element but does not add or remove DOM
An attribute directive can only set inline styles, never listen to events
An attribute directive must always inject `TemplateRef` and `ViewContainerRef`
An attribute directive is applied via an element selector rather than an attribute selector
questions.explanation: An attribute directive changes the appearance or behavior of an existing element/component/directive without adding or removing DOM â€” that is the job of a structural directive, which injects `TemplateRef`/`ViewContainerRef`.

questions.question: You render a list of primitive strings that have no stable id. Which `track` expression is the appropriate choice?
options: `track $index`
`track item.id`
`track Math.random()`
`track $count`
questions.explanation: For primitives with no id, `track $index` is the recommended identity. `item.id` does not exist on a primitive, and an unstable value like `Math.random()` defeats reuse by changing identity every render.

questions.question: When the control-flow migration schematic converts a `*ngFor` that had **no** `trackBy`, what does it use as the `track` expression?
options: `track item` â€” the loop variable itself, which you should review and refine afterward
`track $index` automatically, which is always optimal
It leaves `track` off and emits a TODO comment
It infers `item.id` by inspecting the item's type
questions.explanation: The schematic defaults to `track item` (the loop variable itself) when no `trackBy` existed, and the docs advise reviewing and refining it afterward since the loop variable may not be the best identity.

questions.question: For a component used inside a `@defer` block to actually be code-split into its own lazy chunk, which conditions must hold? (select all that apply)
options: The component must be standalone
It must not be referenced elsewhere in the same file
It must be registered in an NgModule's `declarations`
It must be imported with a dynamic `import()` in the component class
questions.explanation: Deferred dependencies must be standalone and referenced solely inside the `@defer` block in that file; any eager reference forces them into the main bundle. Non-standalone deps cannot be deferred, and `@defer` handles the dynamic import for you.

questions.question: What does `prefetch on idle` accomplish in a `@defer` block whose main trigger is `on viewport`?
options: Downloads the chunk early during browser idle while still showing the placeholder until the viewport trigger fires
Renders the deferred content immediately on idle, ignoring the viewport trigger
Cancels the viewport trigger and loads only when idle
Keeps the `@loading` block visible for at least the idle period
questions.explanation: `prefetch on â€¦` decouples fetch from render: the chunk downloads early on its own trigger while the placeholder stays visible until the main (viewport) trigger fires. It does not render or replace the main trigger.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes `@let` in a template?
options: It is reactive and recomputes on change detection, but is read-only and cannot be reassigned
It is a one-time snapshot that never updates after first render
It can be reassigned later in the template like a mutable JavaScript variable
It is hoisted, so it can be referenced before its declaration
questions.explanation: `@let` is reactive â€” it recomputes whenever its dependencies change â€” but it is read-only and cannot be reassigned. It is also not hoisted; referencing it before declaration is a compile error.

questions.question: Why is `@let user = user$ | async;` followed by reusing `user` across bindings preferred over repeating `user$ | async` in each binding?
options: It subscribes to the stream only once instead of creating multiple subscriptions
It makes `user` mutable so child components can write back to the stream
It forces synchronous resolution of the observable before render
It bypasses change detection for the bound value
questions.explanation: Aliasing the async result with `@let` subscribes a single time and reuses the value, whereas repeating `| async` would create a separate subscription per usage.

questions.question: For toggling a single CSS class based on a boolean, which is the idiomatic modern approach?
options: `[class.active]="isActive()"` â€” a native binding requiring no import
`[ngClass]="{ active: isActive() }"` â€” always preferred for single tokens
Injecting `ElementRef` and writing `classList.toggle` directly
Importing `CommonModule` and using `*ngClass`
questions.explanation: For the single-token case, native `[class.x]` is built into the template compiler (no import) and is the idiomatic choice. `NgClass`/`NgStyle` are reserved for genuinely dynamic maps of names.

questions.question: The directive `host` metadata object (e.g. `host: { '(click)': 'onClick()' }`) and the `@HostListener`/`@HostBinding` decorators are functionally equivalent ways to wire host events and bindings.
questions.explanation: The `host` object and the `@HostListener`/`@HostBinding` decorators are equivalent; the `host` object is the lint-preferred, more declarative form.

questions.question: Given `<div *appUnless="cond">`, what does the leading asterisk desugar to?
options:
`
`

`
` with no template wrapper

`
`

`
` evaluated as a plain attribute string
questions.explanation:
The `*` is pure sugar: it wraps the host element in an `` carrying the directive and moves the microsyntax onto that template as bindings â€” `
`.

questions.question: In `*ngFor="let x of items; trackBy: fn"`, how are the `of` and `trackBy` microsyntax keys translated to directive inputs?
options: They are PascalCased and prefixed with the selector, binding `ngForOf` and `ngForTrackBy`
They bind directly as `of` and `trackBy` inputs on `NgForOf`
They become context variables `$of` and `$trackBy` rather than inputs
They are merged into a single `ngFor` object input
questions.explanation: Microsyntax keys are PascalCased and prefixed with the directive selector, so `of`/`trackBy` bind the inputs `ngForOf` and `ngForTrackBy`.

questions.question: Reading the code, what will the rendered output be?
options: The `name` of the object returned by `user()`, because `$implicit` binds to `let-u`
Nothing, because `let-u` must be written as `let-u="$implicit"` to receive the value
The literal string `user()` since the context is not evaluated
A runtime error, because `ngTemplateOutlet` cannot pass a context object
questions.explanation: The `$implicit` field of the context object surfaces as the bare `let-u`, so `{{ u.name }}` renders the name of the object returned by `user()`. Named (non-implicit) fields would require `let-x="key"`.

questions.question: Which statements about the Directive Composition API (`hostDirectives`) are correct? (select all that apply)
options: Every host directive must be standalone; `standalone: false` is rejected at compile time
A composed directive's inputs/outputs are private by default and must be explicitly listed (and may be aliased) to expose them
Host directives initialize and run their host bindings before the host component, so the host can override them
Composed directives are applied by writing them in the component's template markup
Inputs and outputs of composed directives are automatically re-exposed on the host's public API
questions.explanation: Host directives must be standalone; their inputs/outputs are private unless explicitly listed (and optionally aliased); and they initialize/bind before the host so the host can override. They are applied from TypeScript, not markup, and are not auto-exposed.

questions.question: Reading the code, what happens at the marked line?
options: A compile error, because `label` is scoped to the `@for` view and is out of scope outside it
It renders the `label` of the last iteration, since `@let` leaks to the parent view
It renders an empty string, because `label` is undefined but valid outside the loop
It renders the `label` of the first iteration, which is hoisted to the parent
questions.explanation: `@let` is lexically scoped to its declaring view and descendants only; it is not visible to the parent or sibling views. Referencing `label` outside the `@for` is a compile error, not a runtime empty value.
